title: First-Order Exactness and Methods
subtitle: Exact equations from motivation through intuition, repair of non-exact equations, and physical applications.
description: Exact equations from motivation through intuition, repair of non-exact equations, and physical applications.

atAGlance.title: Unit 7 at a Glance: Core Sub-Topics
atAGlance.summary: A first-order ODE written as $M\,dx + N\,dy = 0$ is \emph{exact} when its left side is already the total differential $dF$ of some scalar potential $F$, making the general solution simply $F(x,y)=C$. This unit develops the complete toolkit: test the cross-partials to detect exactness, reconstruct the potential via the integration loop, repair non-exact equations with special integrating factors, connect the theory to conservative vector fields, and read the physics through thermodynamic state functions.

bullets.body: the criterion $M_y = N_x$ and the implicit general solution $F(x,y)=C$.
Clairaut's theorem: $F_{xy}=F_{yx}$ is why $M_y=N_x$ guarantees a potential.
the integration loop: $F = \int M\,dx + g(y)$, fix $g'(y)$ by matching $F_y = N$.

bullets.lead: 7.4 Non-Exact Equations and Special Integrating Factors
bullets.body: integrating factor $\mu(x)$ when $\Delta/N$ is pure in $x$; $\mu(y)$ when $-\Delta/M$ is pure in $y$ (where $\Delta = M_y - N_x$).
decision tree: test both $\mu(x)$ and $\mu(y)$ routes systematically, then execute the repair and re-verify exactness before reconstructing $F$.

bullets.lead: 7.6 Exact Equations and Conservative Vector Fields
bullets.body: $M_y=N_x$ iff $\mathbf F=\langle M,N\rangle$ is conservative; path independence via the Fundamental Theorem for Line Integrals.
$dU$ is an exact state differential; $\delta Q$ is inexact; the integrating factor $1/T$ produces exact entropy $dS = \delta Q/T$.

modules.title: Exact Differential Equations
modules.topics: A first-order ODE $M(x,y)\,dx + N(x,y)\,dy = 0$ is \emph{exact} on a simply connected region when its left side equals the total differential $dF$ of some potential function $F(x,y)$. The key theorem reduces exactness to a single algebraic test on the cross-partial derivatives of $M$ and $N$.

formulas.title: Exactness Criterion and Implicit Solution
formulas.body:
The ODE $M\,dx + N\,dy = 0$ (with $M,N$ having continuous first partials) is
\strong{exact} on a simply connected region if and only if
\[
  \frac{\partial M}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial N}{\partial x}.
\]
When exact, a potential $F(x,y)$ exists satisfying $F_x = M$ and $F_y = N$,
and the \strong{general solution} is
\[
  F(x,y) = C.
\]

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Swapped Cross Partials
warning.body:
Always differentiate $M$ with respect to \strong{$y$} and $N$ with respect to
\strong{$x$}. The test is $M_y = N_x$, never $M_x$ versus $N_y$. Computing the
same-variable partials tells you nothing about exactness and will produce a
false positive or false negative on every equation you test.

modules.title: Exact Equations Intuition
modules.topics: The cross-partial criterion is not an arbitrary rule --- it is Clairaut's theorem in disguise. If $M = F_x$ and $N = F_y$ for some $F$, then $M_y = F_{xy}$ and $N_x = F_{yx}$, and Clairaut guarantees the two mixed partials agree whenever $F$ has continuous second partials. Exactness is precisely the statement that $M$ and $N$ are partials of a common function.

formulas.title: Clairaut's Theorem and the Exact-Differential Connection
formulas.body:
On a simply connected region with $M,N$ continuously differentiable,
\[
  M\,dx + N\,dy = dF
  \iff
  \frac{\partial M}{\partial y}
  = \frac{\partial^{2}F}{\partial y\,\partial x}
  = \frac{\partial^{2}F}{\partial x\,\partial y}
  = \frac{\partial N}{\partial x}.
\]
The equality of mixed partials is both \strong{necessary} (if $dF$ exists then
the cross partials must agree) and \strong{sufficient} (on a simply connected
domain, equal cross partials guarantee a global potential).

modules.title: Exact Equations Motivation and Theory
modules.topics: Once exactness is confirmed, the potential $F$ is found by a two-step integration loop. Integrating $M$ with respect to $x$ introduces an arbitrary function $g(y)$ in place of a constant; that function is then pinned down by the condition $F_y = N$.

methodology.title: Potential Function Reconstruction: The Integration Loop
steps: \strong{Integrate $M$ in $x$}, holding $y$ fixed: \[ F(x,y) = \int M(x,y)\,dx + g(y). \]

\strong{Differentiate $F$ in $y$} and set the result equal to $N$: \[ \frac{\partial F}{\partial y} = N(x,y) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad g'(y) = N(x,y) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\int M\,dx. \] (All $x$-terms must cancel; if they do not, re-check the exactness test.)

warning.title: Common Pitfall: $g'(y)$ Must Be Free of $x$
warning.body:
After forming $g'(y) = N - \partial_y\!\int M\,dx$, every $x$-term must
cancel. If $x$ survives in $g'(y)$, the equation was not exact (or an
arithmetic error occurred). Integrating an $x$-dependent expression as if it
were a function of $y$ alone produces a wrong answer without any algebra error
to signal the problem.

modules.title: Non-Exact Equations and Special Integrating Factors
modules.topics: When $M_y \neq N_x$, no potential $F$ exists for the equation as written. Multiplying both sides by a non-zero \emph{integrating factor} $\mu$ can change the cross partials of $\mu M$ and $\mu N$ until they match. Two special forms of $\mu$ are algebraically testable and cover the majority of non-exact equations encountered in practice.

formulas.title: Integrating Factors $\mu(x)$ and $\mu(y)$
formulas.body:
Let $\Delta = M_y - N_x$ (the \strong{exactness defect}).
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em, itemsep=4pt]
    \item \strong{Test for $\mu(x)$:} Compute $\dfrac{\Delta}{N}$.
          If the result depends on $x$ \emph{only}, set $h(x)=\Delta/N$ and
          \[
            \mu(x) = \exp\!\left(\int h(x)\,dx\right).
          \]
    \item \strong{Test for $\mu(y)$:} Compute $\dfrac{N_x - M_y}{M}
          = \dfrac{-\Delta}{M}$.
          If the result depends on $y$ \emph{only}, set $k(y)=-\Delta/M$ and
          \[
            \mu(y) = \exp\!\left(\int k(y)\,dy\right).
          \]
\end{itemize}
After multiplying the ODE through by $\mu$, solve the resulting exact equation
via the integration loop (Section~7.3).

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Always Re-Verify Exactness After Multiplying
warning.body:
The formulas for $\mu(x)$ and $\mu(y)$ are derived by assuming the repaired
equation will be exact. After multiplying by $\mu$, explicitly re-compute the
cross partials $(\mu M)_y$ and $(\mu N)_x$ and confirm they are equal before
proceeding. A mis-simplified defect ratio can give a wrong $\mu$ that does
\emph{not} restore exactness.

modules.title: Almost Exact Differential Equations
modules.topics: An \emph{almost exact} equation is non-exact but possesses an integrating factor of one of the two standard forms ($\mu(x)$ or $\mu(y)$). The key skill is recognizing which test succeeds and executing the full repair-and-solve workflow systematically.

formulas.title: Why the Integrating Factor ODE Works
formulas.body:
Requiring $(\mu M)_y = (\mu N)_x$ for $\mu = \mu(x)$ yields
\[
  \mu\,M_y = \mu'\,N + \mu\,N_x
  \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
  \frac{\mu'}{\mu} = \frac{M_y - N_x}{N} = \frac{\Delta}{N},
\]
a separable ODE in $\mu(x)$ whose solution is $\mu = e^{\int \Delta/N\,dx}$.
The analogous calculation with $\mu = \mu(y)$ gives $\mu'/\mu = -\Delta/M$.

methodology.title: Integrating-Factor Decision Tree
steps: \strong{Try $\mu(x)$:} Form $\Delta/N$. Does all $y$ cancel? If yes, $\mu(x) = e^{\int \Delta/N\,dx}$.
\strong{Try $\mu(y)$:} Form $-\Delta/M$. Does all $x$ cancel? If yes, $\mu(y) = e^{\int(-\Delta/M)\,dy}$.
Multiply the ODE by $\mu$, re-verify $(\mu M)_y = (\mu N)_x$, then use the integration loop to find $F$ and write $F = C$.

modules.title: Exact Equations and Conservative Vector Fields
modules.topics: Every exact equation $M\,dx + N\,dy = 0$ can be read as the zero-work condition $\mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r}=0$ for the vector field $\mathbf{F}=\langle M,N\rangle$. Exactness is equivalent to $\mathbf{F}$ being \emph{conservative}, and the potential $F$ of the ODE is the scalar potential $\varphi$ with $\nabla\varphi=\mathbf{F}$.

formulas.title: Conservative Fields, Scalar Potentials, and Path Independence
formulas.body:
A $C^1$ vector field $\mathbf{F}=\langle M,N\rangle$ on a simply connected
region is \strong{conservative} if and only if
\[
  \frac{\partial M}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial N}{\partial x}.
\]
When conservative, there exists a scalar potential $\varphi$ with
$\nabla\varphi = \mathbf{F}$, found by the same integration loop as the ODE
potential. Line integrals are then \strong{path independent}:
\[
  \int_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r}
  = \varphi\!\left(B\right) - \varphi\!\left(A\right)
\]
for any smooth curve $C$ from point $A$ to point $B$ (Fundamental Theorem for
Line Integrals). Equivalently, $\oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r}=0$ for
every closed curve.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: The Domain Must Be Simply Connected
warning.body:
The equivalence $M_y = N_x \iff$ conservative holds only on a
\strong{simply connected} domain (a domain with no holes). On a
multiply-connected domain such as $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{(0,0)\}$, the field
$\mathbf{F}=\langle -y,\,x\rangle/(x^2+y^2)$ satisfies $M_y=N_x$ everywhere
it is defined yet is \emph{not} conservative: its integral around the unit
circle equals $2\pi\neq 0$.

cheat.subtitle: Condensed Cheat Sheet: First-Order Exactness and Methods

takeaway.title: Unit 7 Key Takeaway
takeaway.body:
Every exact equation $M\,dx+N\,dy=0$ reduces to finding the potential
\highlight{$F(x,y)=C$} via the integration loop
\highlight{$F=\int M\,dx+g(y)$} with $g'(y)$ pinned by matching
$F_y=N$. For non-exact equations, compute the defect
\highlight{$\Delta=M_y-N_x$} and apply
\highlight{$\mu(x)=e^{\int\Delta/N\,dx}$} when $\Delta/N$ is
pure in $x$, or \highlight{$\mu(y)=e^{-\int\Delta/M\,dy}$} when
$-\Delta/M$ is pure in $y$; always re-verify exactness after multiplying. The
conservative-field identity \highlight{$M_y=N_x\iff\mathbf{F}=\nabla\varphi$}
makes line integrals path-independent:
\highlight{$\int_C\mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r}=\varphi(B)-\varphi(A)$}.
In thermodynamics, \highlight{$dU$} is exact while
\highlight{$\delta Q$} is not; the integrating factor
\highlight{$1/T$} converts $\delta Q$ into the exact entropy
differential \highlight{$dS=C_V\ln T+nR\ln V+\text{const}$}.
